what_it_does: Provides an implementation guide for building the Registry-Generation Chain: it lays out the sequence of operations, file paths and modification roles, the register_element role schema and context, guiding principles, do's and don'ts, a parallel-loop split, and verification-by-use steps.
observations: The chain is treated as DATA (a saved cascade) rather than a hand-written orchestration script.
Two parallel loops are defined: a guided-review loop [M] and a cognition loop [C], meeting at RG7 (cascade) and RG10 (whole-by-use).
The end-to-end sequence is EXTRACT, GROUND, MAP, REDUCE, CONFIRM, PROPOSE, APPROVE, WRITE-BACK, PROVE.
addresses.json is an operator-only floor: Tim approves, and the chain must never auto-write it.
The register_element role has a concrete output schema: address, represents, howto, capabilities, maps_to_feature, confidence.
Context assembly for register_element includes element outerHTML/text/tag, parent dossier, mockup summary, few-shot entries, and feature inventory.
File paths are tagged NEW/MODIFY/REUSE with assigned loop owners.
Do's/don'ts emphasize no-hardcoding, no-fiction, no parallel systems, and marking un-built elements as proposed.
Verification is specified by concrete use: inspect candidates.json, run cascades, approve batches, reload studio, click once-dead elements.
Coordination relies on a claims board, messages, flag-tiers, and file-disjoint responsibilities.
standouts: Registry-is-truth via a declared, saved cascade instead of a bespoke orchestration script.
Operator-only approval floor with git-revert as the second safety net.
Explicit file-disjoint parallel-loop split between guided-review and cognition loops.
conventions: Uses numbered RG criteria and loop tags [M], [C], [J].
Heavy internal cross-referencing to criteria and files.
Prescriptive do/don't lists and verification-by-use checklists.
concerns: Dense prescriptive detail may drift from actual implementation as files evolve.
Heavy reliance on a single operator (Tim) as the approval bottleneck.
Parallel-loop split assumes strict file-disjoint coordination; overlap is not addressed.
No explicit fallback if the feature inventory is incomplete or stale.
notes: This is the build recipe for a registry-generation pipeline, emphasizing data-driven cascades, grounded context, and operator-gated write-back.
questions: What is the exact schema of candidates.json?
How is the 'meaningful element' filter parameterized and validated?
What is the precise content and schema of the feature inventory?
How do save_cascade and run_cascade work mechanically?
purpose_vs_actual: Yes: it states it is an implementation guide for how to build each RG criterion, and it provides that sequence, roles, schema, and verification steps.
apparent_themes: Operator consent and human approval floor
intention_signals: To prevent a hand-orchestrated, model-hallucinated registry by declaring a reusable, context-heavy, human-approved cascade that turns mockups into grounded, deduplicated, feature-mapped dossiers.
novelty: The explicit 'operator-only floor' consent model combined with a saved cascade and a file-disjoint parallel-loop split between guided-review and cognition loops.
contribution: A detailed, criterion-mapped build plan with role assignments, file-path decisions, a concrete role schema and context template, do/don't guardrails, and use-based verification steps that a fused system would keep as the implementation specification.
summary_for_embedding: Implementation guide for a registry-generation chain that extracts mockup elements, grounds them in summaries and feature inventory, deduplicates and confirms them, surfaces proposals for operator approval, writes back to addresses.json and mockups, and verifies by clicking once-dead elements.
intention_for_embedding: To specify how to build a data-driven, human-gated registry-generation pipeline that turns mockups into a maintained, grounded UI-address registry.
